unit.subject: English
unit.unitTitle: All About Me and My Family
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to your first unit of Year 3 English! In this unit you will learn how to talk about yourself and your family, and about the good values that hold a family and a school together — respect, honour, and duty. You will learn how to behave well in public and in private, and how to talk about health and safety. By the end, you will be ready to introduce your family and share what matters to you, just like Musa does in this unit's story.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day.
Do the six lessons in the Grammar file, one at a time.
Read the Story file, “Musa's Big Day”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.

visual.alt: A learner introducing family members and explaining respectful behaviour

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The people who live with you and love you.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My family has my mum, my dad, my sister, and me.
practiceSentences: My family has my mum, my dad, my sister, and me.
Musa loves eating dinner with his family every evening.
How many people are in your family?
Aisha said, "My family prays together at home."
My family visits Grandma after Friday prayers.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - a - m - i - l - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'family', use it in a sentence, and connect it to All About Me and My Family.

dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My parents take me to school every morning.
practiceSentences: My parents take me to school every morning.
A parent helps children learn right from wrong.
Which parent walks you to school each day?
Yusuf said, "My parent packed dates in my lunch."
I hug each parent before I sleep at night.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - a - r - e - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'parent', use it in a sentence, and connect it to All About Me and My Family.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person you like and spend time with.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My friend Selma and I play football after school.
practiceSentences: My friend Selma and I play football after school.
Samira shared her book with her new friend.
Who is your best friend at school?
Ishmael said, "A good friend always tells the truth."
My friend and I walk to the mosque together.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - r - i - e - n - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'friend', use it in a sentence, and connect it to All About Me and My Family.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who learns in school.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Every student in my class can read now.
practiceSentences: Every student in my class can read now.
Every student lines up quietly before class begins.
Which student watered the class plants today?
The teacher said, "Every student did well today."
I am a proud student at Ehel Academy.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - t - u - d - e - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'student', use it in a sentence, and connect it to All About Me and My Family.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A younger person, or someone in a lower class.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My little brother is a junior in the school choir.
practiceSentences: My little brother is a junior in the school choir.
The junior class eats lunch before the older children.
Is your brother a junior in the reading club?
Aisha said, "I help the junior pupils find their room."
My cousin is a junior, so I guide him.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: j - u - n - i - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'junior', use it in a sentence, and connect it to All About Me and My Family.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: An older person, or someone who has learned more.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The senior student helped the younger children find their room.
practiceSentences: The senior student helped the younger children find their room.
A senior pupil showed the new children around school.
Will the senior class lead the Eid assembly?
Yusuf said, "The senior students read to us on Fridays."
My sister is a senior, so she helps me study.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - e - n - i - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'senior', use it in a sentence, and connect it to All About Me and My Family.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To act in a good and kind way.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Please behave and listen well to your teacher.
practiceSentences: Please behave and listen well to your teacher.
Children who behave well make their teachers happy.
Did you behave kindly at Grandma's house today?
Mum said, "Please behave gently with your baby brother."
I try to behave well at the mosque.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - e - h - a - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'behave', use it in a sentence, and connect it to All About Me and My Family.

dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: His good behaviour made the whole class happy.
practiceSentences: His good behaviour made the whole class happy.
Good behaviour means sharing, listening, and speaking kindly.
Was your behaviour kind during playtime today?
The teacher said, "Your behaviour made me very proud."
My behaviour improves when I remember to be patient.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - e - h - a - v - i - o - u - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'behaviour', use it in a sentence, and connect it to All About Me and My Family.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Being kind and listening to others.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We show respect when we let other people talk.
practiceSentences: We show respect when we let other people talk.
We show respect by listening when others speak.
How do you show respect to your grandparents?
Samira said, "Respect means waiting for your turn."
I show respect when I greet my teacher politely.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - e - s - p - e - c - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'respect', use it in a sentence, and connect it to All About Me and My Family.

dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We honour our parents by helping them at home.
practiceSentences: We honour our parents by helping them at home.
Children honour their parents by obeying them kindly.
How can we honour our grandparents this Eid?
Dad said, "We honour guests by serving them first."
I honour my mother by helping without being asked.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - o - n - o - u - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'honour', use it in a sentence, and connect it to All About Me and My Family.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Something you must do or take care of.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: It is my duty to help my little sister with her bag.
practiceSentences: It is my duty to help my little sister with her bag.
Feeding the cat is Musa's duty every morning.
What duty do you have at home this week?
Aisha said, "My duty is tidying the bookshelf today."
My duty is setting the table for dinner.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'duty', use it in a sentence, and connect it to All About Me and My Family.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: How well your body and your mind feel.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Eating fruit and vegetables is good for your health.
practiceSentences: Eating fruit and vegetables is good for your health.
Sleeping early keeps your health strong for school.
Why is drinking water good for your health?
The doctor said, "Good food protects your health."
I look after my health by playing outside daily.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - e - a - l - t - h
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'health', use it in a sentence, and connect it to All About Me and My Family.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Being safe and not in danger.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We hold hands near the road for our safety.
practiceSentences: We hold hands near the road for our safety.
Safety comes first when we cross the busy road.
Who teaches you about safety at school?
Dad said, "Hold my hand for your safety."
I follow the safety rules in the science room.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - a - f - e - t - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'safety', use it in a sentence, and connect it to All About Me and My Family.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Just for you or a small group, not for everyone.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I write my ideas in my private notebook.
practiceSentences: I write my ideas in my private notebook.
A diary is private, so we do not read it.
Is your bedroom a private place at home?
Samira said, "My drawings are private until they are finished."
I keep my private thoughts in a small notebook.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - r - i - v - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'private', use it in a sentence, and connect it to All About Me and My Family.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: For everyone to see or use.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The public park is open for all the children to play.
practiceSentences: The public park is open for all the children to play.
The public library is open to everyone in town.
Is the big garden public or just for neighbours?
Musa said, "We keep the public park clean."
My family walks in the public park each evening.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - u - b - l - i - c
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'public', use it in a sentence, and connect it to All About Me and My Family.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Talk about the people in your family and what you do together.
Use new words like respect, honour, duty, health, and safety.
Say what is happening now, using the present continuous (“I am helping.”).
Ask Wh- questions to find out about other people.
Give clear instructions using imperatives (“Behave well.”).
Say what you like and don't like, and join ideas with and.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Explain how to behave in public and in private places.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Write simple sentences, questions, a short play, a letter, and instructions.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: My Family
readings.theme: All About Me and My Family
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: My name is Hannah. I live with my family in a small house. My parents are my mum and my dad. I have one brother and one grandmother. We eat dinner together every night. My family helps me, and I help them. It is my duty to keep my room clean and to be kind. I love my family very much.

readings.title: Good Behaviour at School
readings.theme: All About Me and My Family
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: At school, we show respect to our teacher and to our friends. We listen when other people are talking. In a public place, like the lunch area, we sit down, eat nicely, and speak softly. Good behaviour keeps everyone safe and happy. When we honour our teachers and help the younger students, our whole school feels like one big family.

readings.title: Musa Talks About His Family
readings.theme: All About Me and My Family
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “Hello! My name is Musa. I live with my parents, my two sisters, and my grandmother. My parents are my role models. They teach me to help others and to be polite. It is my duty to honour them and to be a good student.”

readings.title: Public and Private
readings.theme: All About Me and My Family
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “In private, at home with your family, you can play and be loud. In public, at school or in the market, you stay calm and speak softly. Behave well, and keep other people safe.”

readings.title: Musa's Big Day
readings.theme: All About Me and My Family
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Musa was not his real name. His real name was Abdullahi. But everyone at school called him Musa, because his father was a teacher and everyone gave the family great respect.
At home, Musa lived with his big, happy family. His parents, his two sisters, and his grandmother all lived together in a small house near the town centre.
One morning, Musa was getting ready for school. His mother said, “Musa, remember to behave in public. Greet your teacher and listen well.”
“I will, Mama,” said Musa. “I am so excited! Today our class is doing a play about health and safety.”
His little sister Samira asked, “What is the play about?”
“It is about staying safe at school and showing respect,” Musa explained.
At school, the students were busy. Some were practising their lines. Some were fixing the props with paper and paint. Musa put on a yellow vest and a hat he had made from old newspaper.
He whispered to his best friend, Adam, “What is your duty in the play?”
“I am playing the senior student,” said Adam. “You have to teach me how to act in public and private places.”
“Oh yes!” said Musa. “That is my job!”
The teacher clapped her hands. “Alright, everyone. We are starting now.” The classroom became quiet, and Musa stepped forward.
“Hello, Senior Student!” he said in a big voice. “This is how you behave in the lunch area. Sit down, eat nicely, and speak softly.”
“Why should I do that?” asked Adam, pretending to be rude.
Musa replied, “Because we must show honour and respect. Behave well, and keep other people safe.”
Then another student walked in and shouted loudly. Musa smiled and said, “In private, you can be loud with your family. But in public, we stay calm.” The whole class laughed. They loved the play.
After the play, the teacher smiled. “Who are your role models?” she asked the class.
Musa raised his hand. “My parents,” he said. “They teach me to help others and to be polite. I want to honour them.”
The teacher nodded. “That is a wonderful answer, Musa.”
Later that day, Musa saw a junior student drop her school bag near the gate. Her books spilled onto the ground.
He ran over at once. “Let me help you,” he said kindly.
“Thank you!” said the girl, smiling. “You are very respectful.”  Musa smiled back. “I am trying to be.”
On the way home, Musa and Adam walked together. “I liked your line in the play,” said Adam. “‘Behave well, and keep others safe.’ That was clever.”
Musa grinned. “We should make posters for school,” he said. “Like ‘Be safe in public’ or ‘Use kind words.’”
“That is a great idea,” said Adam. “Let us ask the teacher tomorrow.”
That evening at home, Musa was writing in his notebook. His grandmother came in. “What are you doing?” she asked.
“I am writing about my duty,” he said. “To help others and to be a good student.” He showed her what he had written:
“At school, I behave with respect. In public, I speak softly. In private, I relax with my family. My duty is to learn, to help, and to stay healthy.”
His mother peeked into the room. “Time for bed,” she said gently.
“Okay, Mama,” said Musa. “Tomorrow I will keep being my best.”
“Good,” she said. “Because every day is a new chance to do your duty.”
Musa closed his eyes and smiled. He liked his nickname now. It reminded him that he was still learning, but growing up with honour. And maybe one day, he would be a senior student that others could look up to.

grammar.title: Present Continuous  (what is happening now)
grammar.explanation: We use the present continuous to talk about actions that are happening right now. It helps us say exactly what people are doing at this moment, like “The student is showing respect.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use two parts: the correct form of be (am / is / are) + the verb with -ing.
I am + verb-ing:  “I am learning English.”
He / She / It is + verb-ing:  “My family is talking about safety.”
We / You / They are + verb-ing:  “They are helping their parents.”
Spelling tip: if a verb ends in e, drop the e (make → making). If a short verb ends in one vowel and one consonant, double the consonant (run → running).
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “They helping their parents.”  ✗
Fix: don't forget the be word: “They are helping their parents.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: If you can say “right now” after the sentence, use the present continuous. “She is reading — right now!”
grammar.practice: Write the correct form: use am / is / are and add -ing to the verb in brackets. | The children ______ (play) outside. | She ______ (eat) her lunch. | We ______ (learn) about health. | He ______ (do) his homework. | My family ______ (talk) about safety.

grammar.title: Wh- Questions  (asking for information)
grammar.explanation: Wh- questions begin with who, what, where, when, why, and how. Good learners ask questions to find out more about people and the world.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Who → a person:  “Who is your best friend?”
What → a thing or idea:  “What is your duty at school?”
Where → a place:  “Where do you play after school?”
When → a time:  “When do you have lunch?”
Why → a reason:  “Why do you like reading?”
How → the way:  “How do you get to school?”
Always finish a question with a question mark ( ? ).
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “What your name?”  ✗   (the verb is missing)
Fix: “What is your name?”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Wh- words are like keys that unlock answers. Pick the right key: When = time, Where = place, Who = person.
grammar.practice: Write the correct Wh- word for each question. | ______ do you have lunch? (a time) | ______ is your favourite subject? (a thing) | ______ is your best friend? (a person) | ______ do you get to school? (the way)

grammar.title: Giving Instructions  (imperatives)
grammar.explanation: When we tell someone what to do, we use the imperative. It gives a clear instruction or rule, like “Respect your parents” or “Behave well in public.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start with the base verb — no ending:  “Listen to your teacher.”
You do not need to say “you”. It is already understood:  “Behave well” means “You behave well.”
Add please to be polite:  “Please help your friends.”
To say NOT to do something, use don't:  “Don't be rude.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “You respects your parents.”  ✗
Fix: no “you”, and use the base verb: “Respect your parents.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of the word command. An imperative gives one clear command to follow — short and strong.
grammar.practice: Complete each instruction with the base verb in brackets. Add “Don't” where it says (not). | ______ (listen) to your teacher. | ______ (not / disturb) others while they are working. | ______ (help) your classmates. | ______ (be) kind to everyone.

grammar.title: Action Verbs  (doing words)
grammar.explanation: Action verbs tell us what someone is doing, like help, learn, run, and play. They make your sentences come alive: instead of “They are,” you can say “They are helping their parents.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Find the subject (who is doing it):  “She is learning about health.”
Add the doing word (the action verb):  learning, helping, running.
For he / she / it in the simple present, add -s:  “He runs fast.”  “She helps her mum.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “He run fast.”  ✗
Fix: for he / she / it, add -s to the verb: “He runs fast.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Action = Doing. If someone is doing something, the word for it is an action verb.
grammar.practice: Write the correct form of the verb in brackets. | She ______ (play) the drum beautifully. | They ______ (help) their friends every day. | I ______ (learn) new words every day. | He ______ (run) fast in the race.

grammar.title: Expressing Likes and Dislikes
grammar.explanation: We talk about what we enjoy and what we do not enjoy. This helps us share our feelings, like “I like being with my family” or “He doesn't like public speaking.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use like with I, you, we, they. Use likes with he, she, it.
“I like helping my parents.”
“She likes reading books.”
To say you do NOT enjoy something, use don't (I / you / we / they) or doesn't (he / she / it).
“We don't like loud noise.”
“He doesn't like getting up late.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “He like football.”  ✗
Fix: add the “s” for he / she / it: “He likes football.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: I / you / we / they → like.   He / she / it → likes.   Don't want it? Put “not” after do or does.
grammar.practice: Write like, likes, don't, or doesn't. | I ______ eating dates in the evening. | She ______ going to the park with her family. | They ______ (negative) like loud shouting in public. | He ______ (negative) like reading in a noisy room.

grammar.title: The Conjunction “and”
grammar.explanation: A conjunction is a joining word. And joins two ideas or things together, so we can say two things at once, like “Honour and duty are both important.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Join two words:  “Health and safety go together.”
Join two ideas:  “I help my mum and I listen to my teacher.”
In a list of three, put a comma, then and:  “I like reading, drawing, and football.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I like apples I like oranges.”  ✗  (two ideas, no joining word)
Fix: join them with and: “I like apples and I like oranges.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of “and” as a bridge between two islands. Each island is one idea; the bridge joins them.
grammar.practice: Join the ideas with “and”. | I respect my parents ______ my teachers. | We must be kind ______ safe at school. | My family talks ______ laughs at dinner.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Introduce Your Family
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Get ready to say:
“In my family, there is ______, ______, and me.”
“My ______ helps me to ______.”
“Together, my family likes to ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you name the people clearly? Did you speak loudly enough?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise introduce your family and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — What Do You Like?
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
“I like ______, and I like ______.”
“I don't like ______.”
“My best friend likes ______.”  (Remember: he/she likes — with an “s”!)
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise what do you like? and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — Ask Me a Question
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person asks a Wh- question: “Who is in your family?” or “What is your duty at home?”
The other person answers in a full sentence.
Then swap turns. Try to use who, what, where, when, why, and how.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise ask me a question and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — One-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a one-minute talk using an opening, three connected details, and a closing sentence.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise one-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1 — Simple Sentences About You
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Finish these sentences, then write two more of your own:
I like helping my ______.
In my family, I ______.
She is learning about ______.
My duty at home is to ______.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about simple sentences about you.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 2 — Write Some Questions
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a Wh- question you could ask a new friend about each of these:
their family  →  “What ______?”
how they show respect at school  →  “How ______?”
what they like to do  →  “What ______?”
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about write some questions.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 3 — A Short Play Script
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a tiny play (four lines) where a junior student teaches a senior student about duty, like Musa does in the story. Use this shape:
Musa:  ______________________
Senior:  ______________________
Musa:  ______________________
Senior:  ______________________
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about a short play script.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 4 — A Letter to a New Friend
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a short letter to a new friend. Tell them about your family and about one value that matters to you, such as respect or honour. Use this shape:
Dear ______, / My name is ______. In my family there is ______. I think ______ is important because ______. Your friend, ______
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about a letter to a new friend.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Instructions
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write three instructions (imperatives) for one of these titles: “How to behave in public” OR “How to stay safe at school”. Start each line with a base verb, like “Speak softly.”
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about instructions.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 6 — Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about the unit. Begin with a topic sentence and join ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about connected paragraph.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

activities.title: Activities 1 — True or False (About Values)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
We show respect by shouting over other people.
A parent is a mother or a father.
In a public place, we should behave well and speak softly.
A duty is something you must do.
Answers: 1. False (we listen quietly)  2. True  3. True  4. True
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2 — Match the Word to Its Meaning
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each word to its meaning.
honour   →   (a) being safe and not in danger
safety   →   (b) something you must do
duty     →   (c) to show someone great respect
Answers: 1 → c,  2 → a,  3 → b
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3 — Fill in the Blanks
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Use like, likes, public, or private.
The park is a ______ place where everyone can play.
My diary is ______; it is just for me.
I ______ helping my family.
She ______ reading books at night.
Answers: 1. public  2. private  3. like  4. likes
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4 — Present Continuous
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write what is happening now. Use is / are + verb-ing.
The students ______ (practise) their play.
My family ______ (talk) about safety.
He ______ (help) the younger student.
Answers: 1. are practising  2. is talking  3. is helping
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5 — Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6 — Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six important details in the unit texts. Record the detail and explain what it helps the reader understand.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: All About Me and My Family learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit Writing 1 — Simple Sentences About You and a recording of Speaking 1 — Introduce Your Family. Improve both pieces using the Grade 3 rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'family' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who learns in school. | A person you like and spend time with. | A younger person, or someone in a lower class. | The people who live with you and love you.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The people who live with you and love you.
quizzes.explanation: My family has my mum, my dad, my sister, and me.

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'parent' mean?
quizzes.options: A mother or a father. | A person who learns in school. | A younger person, or someone in a lower class. | An older person, or someone who has learned more.
quizzes.explanation: My parents take me to school every morning.

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'friend' mean?
quizzes.options: A person you like and spend time with. | A younger person, or someone in a lower class. | An older person, or someone who has learned more. | To act in a good and kind way.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person you like and spend time with.
quizzes.explanation: My friend Selma and I play football after school.

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'student' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who learns in school. | An older person, or someone who has learned more. | The way someone acts. | To act in a good and kind way.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person who learns in school.
quizzes.explanation: Every student in my class can read now.

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Good Behaviour at School': When we honour our teachers and help the younger students, our whole school feels like one big _____.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: When we honour our teachers and help the younger students, our whole school feels like one big family.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Musa's Big Day': He whispered to his best _____, Adam, “What is your duty in the play?” “I am playing the senior student,” said Adam.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: He whispered to his best friend, Adam, “What is your duty in the play?” “I am playing the senior student,” said Adam.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Musa Talks About His Family': It is my duty to honour them and to be a good _____.”
quizzes.explanation: The text says: It is my duty to honour them and to be a good student.”
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Musa's Big Day': “That is a wonderful answer, Musa.” Later that day, Musa saw a _____ student drop her school bag near the gate.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “That is a wonderful answer, Musa.” Later that day, Musa saw a junior student drop her school bag near the gate.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Musa's Big Day': “Hello, _____ Student!” he said in a big voice.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “Hello, Senior Student!” he said in a big voice.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Public and Private': _____ well, and keep other people safe.”
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Behave well, and keep other people safe.”
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you read the family text with me and explain any hard words?”
“Can you play a listening game about public and private places?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me practise introducing my family?”
“Can you ask me Wh- questions about myself?”
“Can you check that I say ‘likes’ correctly for he and she?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check if my sentences are correct?”
“Can you help me write my letter to a new friend?”
“Can you read my play script with me, using different voices?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me talk about the people in my family?”
“What is the difference between a junior and a senior?”
“Can you give me more sentences with the word ______?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“What is the difference between behave and behaviour?”
“Can you help me think of ways to show respect at school?”
“Can you check my sentence about my duty at home?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on the difference between private and public?”
“Can you help me make a short list of safety rules for school?”
“What can I do to keep good health?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my grammar answers?”
“Why do we say ‘he likes’ but ‘I like’?”
“Can you give me five more present continuous sentences to practise?”

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: The people who live with you and love you. My family has my mum, my dad, my sister, and me.
A mother or a father. My parents take me to school every morning.
A person you like and spend time with. My friend Selma and I play football after school.
A person who learns in school. Every student in my class can read now.
A younger person, or someone in a lower class. My little brother is a junior in the school choir.
An older person, or someone who has learned more. The senior student helped the younger children find their room.
To act in a good and kind way. Please behave and listen well to your teacher.
The way someone acts. His good behaviour made the whole class happy.
Being kind and listening to others. We show respect when we let other people talk.
To show someone great respect. We honour our parents by helping them at home.
